gaygent_header: THE GAYGENT'S OFFICIAL SURVEILLANCE REPORT — Round of 64 Final
hot_take: The Round of 64 is in the books and the carnage is EXQUISITE. AJ Dybantsa dropped 35 for BYU and then went HOME. High Point — a school whose mascot I refuse to Google — sent Wisconsin packing 83-82. Three managers lost three players each and are pretending they planned this. The tournament chose violence and The Gaygent chose to WATCH.
bust_roast: Nate Ament averaged 17.5 points per game all season for Tennessee, scored ZERO in the Round of 64, and Alex Magged still has 8 active players because that's the kind of man Alex is — he doesn't need every player to show up, he needs the SPREADSHEET to work. Tavari Johnson (20.1 PPG, scored 4) and Tyler Bilodeau (17.6 PPG, scored 0) are also competing for Biggest Disappointment but Ament's zero on a 6-seed is the kind of performance that makes you question whether he was even IN the building.

managers.AJ Spile: AJ Spile is leading this pool with 173 points and THREE dead players and I need everyone to sit with that for a moment. Three eliminated. 62% survival rate. Only 63% of his points are coming from players who are still ALIVE. And he's STILL in first place by 29 points. AJ Dybantsa dropped 35 — the highest individual score in the entire pool, tied with Oweh — and then BYU lost and Dybantsa went home. Mark Mitchell put up 19 for Missouri before getting bounced. Bruce Thornton scored 10 for Ohio State before they got eliminated. That's 64 points from dead men walking. But here's the thing: Labaron Philon (29), Ja'Kobi Gillespie (29), Ryan Conwell (18), Milan Momcilovic (17), and David Punch (16) are all still breathing and all scored double digits. AJ built a roster that can lose 38% of itself and still sit on the throne. That's not strategy. That's AUDACITY.
managers.Alex Magged: I despise this man and his perfect little spreadsheet and his perfect little 100% survival rate and his perfect little 144 points with EIGHT out of EIGHT players still alive. Not ONE eliminated. While the rest of this pool is attending funerals, Alex is hosting a dinner party where all eight guests showed up and six of them brought wine. David Mirkovic dropped 29 on a 13.4 PPG average — that's a 116% overperformance and I KNOW Alex has already calculated that percentage. Dailyn Swain put up 27 across two games including 13 in the First Four that everyone forgot about until The Gaygent investigated. Trey Kaufman-Renn had 25. Morez Johnson had 21. Even Boogie Fland — who I personally wrote off at 11.6 PPG — scored 16. The only player who didn't show up is Nate Ament with ZERO points on 6-seed Tennessee. Zero. But Alex doesn't care because he has SEVEN other players carrying the load and a survival rate that would make a cockroach jealous.
managers.Brandon Nye: Brandon 'I Read The Analytics' Nye is in third with 131 points, five active players, and the emotional wreckage of watching High Point — HIGH POINT — eliminate John Blackwell and Wisconsin 83-82. Blackwell scored 22 beautiful points in that loss. Twenty-two points that now sit in the 'dead weight' column alongside Robert Wright III's 14 (BYU eliminated) and Tavari Johnson's 4 (Akron demolished by Texas Tech 91-71). Four points. Johnson averaged 20.1 ALL SEASON. The Cinderella lottery ticket wasn't even a scratch-off — it was a receipt from CVS. But the living portfolio is doing work: Tyler Tanner led with 26, Flemings and Wagler both put up 18 on Houston and Illinois respectively, Bennett Stirtz had 16 for Iowa, and Bryce Hopkins contributed 13 on St. Johns. 69% of Brandon's points are still live. Nice percentage. Very on brand for this pool.
managers.Mark Weiner: Oh, Mark. My sweet, MUSCULAR Mark. CAYDEN BOOZER SCORED NINETEEN POINTS. I need to say that again because no one in this group chat believed it was possible. Cayden Boozer — the man who averaged 7.1 PPG all season, the man I personally compared to ordering the most expensive bottle at a restaurant and then drinking water — scored 19 points in the Round of 64. That's a 168% overperformance. Duke's second Boozer outscored Thomas Haugh (14) on 1-seed Florida. Mark has 129 points, 6 of 8 alive, and a 92% live portfolio. Malik Reneau led with 24 on Miami FL, Alex Karaban had 22 on UConn, Meleek Thomas put up 21 for Arkansas, and Aday Mara matched Cayden with 19 on Michigan. The losses? Paulius Murauskas — 18.8 PPG all season — scored 4 points before Saint Mary's went home, and TJ Power contributed 6 on 14-seed Penn before getting bounced. Mark lost his two worst investments and kept his six best. Those arms carry portfolios AND basketball rosters apparently.
managers.Jake Pollack: The Toyota Camry took a dent. Nick Boyd — Jake's best player, 20.6 PPG, the man who was supposed to be the engine of this portfolio — dropped 27 points in Wisconsin's loss to High Point and is now eliminated. Twenty-seven dead points. Boopie Miller scored 15 in the First Four before SMU lost to Miami OH — another 15 dead. That's 42 points from dead players. Jake has 128 total but only 67% from active players. The living roster is steady though: Christian Anderson (18), Coen Carr (17), Emanuel Sharp (16), Alex Condon (13), Tre White (12), and Thijs De Ridder (10) — all six scored double digits, all six still alive, spread across six different teams. That's the Camry doing what the Camry does. It lost a headlight and a side mirror but it's still getting you to work. Jake Pollack is the most reliably fine manager in this pool and I mean that as the complicated compliment it is.
managers.Jordan Mackler: Jordan Mackler has EIGHT out of EIGHT players alive and the self-cannibalization prophecy from the draft report remains a ticking time bomb that has not yet detonated. Otega Oweh went absolutely NUCLEAR with 35 points for Kentucky — tied with Dybantsa for the highest individual score in the entire pool. Darius Acuff had 24 for Arkansas. Pryce Sandfort put up 23 for Nebraska. That's 82 points from three players alone. But here's the shadow: Joshua Jefferson scored 2 points in 2 minutes for Iowa State. TWO. On a 16.9 PPG average. Braylon Mullens and Patrick Ngongba both scored ZERO. Jordan has 105 points and a 100% survival rate but three of his eight players contributed 2 points or less combined. The cannon is loaded with three live shells and five blanks. And the Iowa State-Kentucky collision in R32? That's Jordan's Jefferson vs Jordan's Oweh. One of them dies this weekend. The self-cannibalization begins NOW.
managers.Josh Ehrlich: Josh Ehrlich has 96 points, eight players alive, 100% survival, and the energy of a man who showed up to a costume party in business casual. Braden Smith carried with 26 on Purdue — nearly doubling his 14.0 PPG average, the biggest overperformance on Josh's roster. Trevon Brazile had 19 for Arkansas. Rueben Chinyelu contributed 14 on Florida, Sam Lewis had 12 on Virginia. After that it drops to single digits: Lendeborg and Stojakovic both had 9, Bradley had 7. And Tyler Bilodeau — 17.6 PPG all season on UCLA — scored ZERO. Not one point. Josh is the definition of 'present but not particularly engaged.' Everyone's alive, most of them scored, none of them scored enough to make anyone nervous. Josh Ehrlich's roster is a group project where one person did most of the work and everyone else put their name on it.
managers.Nick Reskin: The Cameron Boozer situation needs to be addressed. Nick's data shows 'Cam Boozer' with 0 points, seed 0, team blank. Duke has played. Cayden Boozer scored 19 for Mark Weiner. Cameron Boozer — the NPOY candidate, 22.5 PPG, the projected 115-point nuclear warhead — is a ghost in this dataset. When that gets resolved, Nick's total changes SIGNIFICANTLY. But even with that mystery, the man Nick SHOULD be talking about is Tarris Reed Jr. Reed dropped 31 POINTS for UConn — the third-highest individual score in the entire pool. Thirty-one. On a 13.7 PPG average. That's a 126% overperformance carrying this roster. Amani Lyles scored 26 for Akron before getting eliminated — contributing points from beyond the grave. Fletcher Loyer (14), Duke Miles (13), Jaxon Kohler (12) round it out. Nick has 96 points, 7 of 8 alive, and 73% live. When the Boozer data catches up, this roster jumps. Nick Reskin is either a genius waiting to be revealed or a man with an extremely expensive data error.
managers.Schaf Chulay: Schaf. My darling. My OBSESSION. You are in ninth place and Darryn Peterson — the man with the medical chart longer than his stat sheet, the man who missed 11 games this season, the man whose injury history reads like a Grey's Anatomy episode — just scored 28 POINTS for Kansas. TWENTY-EIGHT. The Peterson Problem from the draft report SOLVED ITSELF. Brayden Burries had 18 on 1-seed Arizona, Milos Uzan contributed 12 on Houston, Malik Thomas had 11 on Virginia, and Solo Ball — whose name I will never stop enjoying — added 9 on UConn. Seven of eight alive, 92% live portfolio. The only casualty is Nolan Winter, who scored 8 before Wisconsin got upset by High Point. But here's what's quietly dangerous: Schaf has players on Arizona (1), Kansas (4), Houston (2), Iowa State (2), UConn (2), and Virginia (3). Six players on seeds 1-4. If the chalk holds this weekend, Schaf's players collectively play the MOST minutes of any roster in R32. The obsession is warranted. Schaf is a sleeper and I am NOT sleeping.
managers.Mike Haughey: Mike Haughey is in last place with 94 points and I genuinely don't understand how this is possible. Eight players. All alive. 100% survival. 100% live portfolio. Every single point on this roster is coming from an active player. And yet — dead last. The problem isn't survival, it's ceiling. Graham Ike led with 19 on Gonzaga. Koa Peat had 15 on Arizona. After that it's Ejiofor (14), Sellers (11), Klavzar (11), Lee (10), Krivas (9), Cadeau (5). Nobody scored 20. Nobody had a breakout game. Nobody made you look up from your phone. Mike has TWO players on Arizona, TWO on St. Johns, and TWO on Florida — three teams, six players. If Arizona, St. Johns, and Florida all make deep runs, Mike's total could DOUBLE through the tournament. But if any of those three lose in R32, Mike loses two players at once. The double-exposure is either brilliant insurance or a house of cards. Mike Haughey is playing the long game and the long game is currently losing.
